title: Modernising our approach to the 2028 Census: Summary of customer and community engagement | Stats NZ
content:
## Modernising our approach to the 2028 Census: Summary of customer and community engagement
18 June 2025, 10:15am
Modernising our approach to the 2028 Census: Summary of customer and community engagement summarises the feedback we received during our public and stakeholder engagement on proposals to modernise our approach to the next census.
Download the full report in PDF or Word format or read a summary below.
- Modernising our approach to the 2028 Census: Summary of customer and community engagement Word document, 325 KB

### Modernising our approach to the 2028 Census: Summary of customer and community engagement
Word document, 325 KB
- Modernising our approach to the 2028 Census: Summary of customer and community engagement Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 430 KB

### Modernising our approach to the 2028 Census: Summary of customer and community engagement
Adobe Acrobat PDF file, 430 KB
This report highlights insights gathered from engagements between January and June 2024. More than 150 agencies, organisations, and individuals provided feedback about the future of Stats NZ’s census and the use of administrative data (admin data), through our engagement teams.

### Summary of key themes
This is a summary of the key themes that emerged from the feedback we received. Many themes covered a wide variety of views.
Most customers agreed that census is useful and provides important information, often through its single-point-in-time nature. A minority believed the census is a nice-to-have.
Some customers questioned cost as a motivator for change, highlighting the impacts of change or other drivers for change, such as data sovereignty.

#### Data collection concerns and considerations
Customers focused on standardising systems and processes involved in collecting data. Several customers expressed concerns about the resilience of admin data over time and hard-to-reach populations.

#### Data quality concerns and considerations
Many customers were concerned about loss of detail and coverage with a change to an admin data-first census. The potential for sampling errors, breaks in the time series, and maintaining data quality were highlighted.

#### Equity considerations
Customers expressed concern for the representation of specific population groups in an admin data-first census.
Most customers urged caution in changing the census model. Many customers felt planned change would occur too fast.

#### Need for relevant and responsive data
Some customers saw change as an opportunity to improve census, mentioning improved frequency and timeliness of data.

#### Responsible management of data
Customers expressed concerns around the management of data, including the governance of data, use of data beyond its original purpose, and the privacy and security of data.

#### Engagement and partnership
Some customers requested further engagement and partnership with their communities. Many felt that partnering with communities to collect data and communicate census results would be beneficial to the success of a new model.
Overall customer sentiment can be described as cautious, with a mix of positive and negative feedback regarding change. This report provides a comprehensive summary of the feedback received during the engagement.
